questions.question: The Sarkaria Commission primarily focussed on strengthening which type of federalism in India?
questions.solution:
Co-operative Federalism and Sarkaria Commission

The Sarkaria Commission (1983) was appointed to examine the relationship between the Centre and the States. Its primary objective was to strengthen co-operative federalism, recommending that the Union should consult States before legislating on Concurrent List subjects and that the office of the Governor should not be misused for political ends.

questions.question: Who acts as the real executive head in a state?
questions.solution:
Real vs. Nominal Executive

In the Indian parliamentary system at the state level, the Governor is the nominal (titular) head, while the Chief Minister is the real executive head. The Chief Minister heads the Council of Ministers, which wields the actual executive power of the state.

questions.question: Which of the following acts was aimed at establishing a federal polity in India?
questions.solution:
Government of India Act, 1935

The Government of India Act, 1935 was a significant milestone that aimed to establish a federal polity in India. It provided for the establishment of an All-India Federation consisting of provinces and princely states as units, and divided powers into Federal, Provincial, and Concurrent lists.

questions.question: The Rajya Sabha seat distribution among states is found in which Schedule of the Indian Constitution?
questions.solution:
Fourth Schedule: Seat Distribution

The Fourth Schedule of the Indian Constitution contains provisions for the allocation of seats in the Rajya Sabha (Council of States) to the various States and Union Territories. The allocation is primarily based on the population of each state.

questions.question: Which Article allows the President to appoint a Commission to investigate the condition of socially and educationally backward classes?
questions.solution:
Article 340: Backward Classes Commission

Article 340 empowers the President to appoint a Commission to investigate the conditions of socially and educationally backward classes. The recommendations of such commissions (like the Kalelkar or Mandal Commission) help the government formulate policies for their upliftment.

questions.question: Provision for securing just and humane conditions of work and maternity relief comes under which Article of the Indian Constitution?
questions.solution:
Article 42: Maternity Relief

Article 42 of the Directive Principles of State Policy mandates that the State shall make provision for securing just and humane conditions of work and for maternity relief. This article is the constitutional basis for laws like the Maternity Benefit Act.

questions.question: What was one recommendation of the Sarkaria Commission regarding Governors?
questions.option_4: Governors should be elected by States.
questions.solution:
Non-Partisan Governors

One of the key recommendations of the Sarkaria Commission was that a Governor should be a non-partisan individual. Specifically, the commission suggested that the appointee should be an eminent person from outside the state who has not been active in politics in the recent past.

questions.question: Which amendment abolished the privy purse and privileges of the former rulers of princely states?
questions.solution:
26th Amendment: Privy Purses

The 26th Constitutional Amendment Act (1971) abolished the privy purses and special privileges that were previously granted to the former rulers of princely states at the time of independence and integration into India.

questions.question: Which of the following is a non-statutory and non-constitutional body?
questions.solution:
NITI Aayog: Non-Constitutional Body

NITI Aayog (National Institution for Transforming India) is a non-statutory and non-constitutional body. It was created via an executive resolution of the Union Cabinet in 2015, replacing the Planning Commission. Unlike the NHRC (statutory) or Attorney General (constitutional), it has no specific act or article backing its existence.

questions.question: Which of the following digital initiatives aims to make the legislative proceedings of State and Union Territory Legislatures in India completely paperless?
questions.solution:
NeVA: Paperless Legislatures

The National e-Vidhan Application (NeVA) is a mission-mode project under the Digital India theme. Its goal is to make all the State and UT Legislatures paperless by digitizing and making relevant documents and proceedings available on a unified platform.

questions.question: Which of the following is a distinct feature of a statutory corporation in India?
questions.option_1: It is created by a special Act, has independent legal status and can sue/be sued.
questions.option_2: It is fully owned by private shareholders and receives no government funding.
questions.option_3: It has no legal existence separate from the government.
questions.option_4: Its employees are considered government civil servants under the usual rules.
questions.solution:
Statutory Corporations

A statutory corporation is a public enterprise created by a special Act of Parliament or State Legislature. It possesses an independent legal status, meaning it can enter into contracts and sue or be sued in its own name, separate from the government.

questions.question: In which case did the SC hold that judicial review is a basic feature of the Constitution?
questions.solution:
Judicial Review as Basic Structure

In the Kesavananda Bharati case (1973), the Supreme Court established that Judicial Review is a part of the 'Basic Structure' of the Constitution. This means that Parliament cannot take away the power of the High Courts and the Supreme Court to review the constitutionality of laws.

questions.question: Which of the following states are covered under the Fifth Schedule of the Indian Constitution?
questions.solution:
Fifth Schedule States

The Fifth Schedule applies to the administration of Scheduled Areas in states other than those in the Northeast (AMTM). States like Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh, Odisha, and Madhya Pradesh have large tribal populations and fall under these provisions to protect tribal land and culture.

questions.question: Who has the authority to transfer judges of the High Courts in India?
questions.option_1: Chief Justice of India acting on behalf of the Supreme Court
questions.solution:
Transfer of High Court Judges

Under Article 222, the President of India has the authority to transfer a judge from one High Court to any other High Court. This power is exercised after consultation with the Chief Justice of India.

questions.question: Which of the following constitutional bodies was created to recommend appointments and transfers of judges in India?
questions.solution:
National Judicial Appointments Commission (NJAC)

The 99th Amendment Act aimed to replace the Collegium system with the National Judicial Appointments Commission (NJAC) to recommend the appointment and transfer of judges. However, the Supreme Court declared it unconstitutional in 2015, restoring the Collegium system.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the Council of Ministers is correct?
questions.option_1: All members of the Council of Ministers are part of the Cabinet.
questions.option_2: Ministers of State rank higher than Cabinet Ministers.
questions.option_3: The Council of Ministers is appointed by the Chief Justice.
questions.option_4: Cabinet Ministers are a smaller group within the Council of Ministers.
questions.solution:
Cabinet vs. Council of Ministers

The Cabinet Ministers are a smaller, senior group within the larger Council of Ministers. While the Council of Ministers consists of all categories (Cabinet, Ministers of State, and Deputy Ministers), only the Cabinet rank ministers attend regular cabinet meetings to decide top-level policy.

questions.question: The provision of early childhood care and education to children below six years of age was introduced by which Constitutional Amendment?
questions.solution:
86th Amendment: Early Childhood Care

The 86th Amendment Act (2002) changed the subject matter of Article 45. It directed the State to provide early childhood care and education for all children until they complete the age of six years (after which Article 21A takes over for formal schooling).

questions.question: The RTI Act applies to which of the followings in India?
questions.solution:
Scope of RTI Act

The Right to Information (RTI) Act, 2005, applies to all public authorities. This includes bodies owned, controlled, or substantially financed by the government, covering the executive, legislature, and to a limited extent, the administrative side of the judiciary.

questions.question: Which of the following falls under the Supreme Court’s original jurisdiction, according to the Constitution of India (Article 131)?
questions.option_1: Enforcement of fundamental rights of individuals
questions.option_2: Appeals from criminal cases in High Courts
questions.option_3: Disputes between States or between a State and the Union
questions.option_4: Election petitions against sitting Members of Parliaments
questions.solution:
Article 131: Original Jurisdiction

Original Jurisdiction means the power to hear a case for the first time. Under Article 131, this is reserved for federal disputes, such as disputes between the Government of India and one or more States, or disputes between two or more States.

questions.question: When was the States Reorganisation Act passed, implementing the Commission’s recommendations?
questions.solution:
States Reorganisation Act, 1956

The States Reorganisation Act was passed in 1956 following the recommendations of the Fazl Ali Commission. It led to the creation of 14 states and 6 union territories, largely based on linguistic boundaries.

questions.question: Which Article of the Indian Constitution provides for the Council of Ministers at the Union level?
questions.solution:
Article 74: Union Council of Ministers

Article 74 provides for a Council of Ministers with the Prime Minister at the head to aid and advise the President. The article further clarifies that the President shall act in accordance with such advice.

questions.question: Which of the following is true regarding the tenure and independence of the Chief Election Commissioner (CEC) of India?
questions.option_1: The CEC can only be removed like a Supreme Court judge by a two-thirds majority in Parliament.
questions.option_2: The other Election Commissioners can only be removed by the CEC, not by the government.
questions.option_3: The CEC’s removal requires assent of the Chief Justice of India but not necessarily the Parliament’s approval.
questions.option_4: The CEC enjoys a fixed tenure of ten years and cannot be replaced before that term under any circumstances.
questions.solution:
Independence of the EC

To ensure independence, the Constitution provides that the other Election Commissioners (ECs) cannot be removed from office except on the recommendation of the Chief Election Commissioner (CEC). This protects the junior commissioners from arbitrary removal by the executive.

questions.question: In which year was the Indian Civil Service (ICS) officially replaced by the Indian Administrative Service (IAS)?
questions.solution:
Replacement of ICS with IAS

The transition from the colonial services to the Indian services occurred in 1947. Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel, the 'Iron Man of India,' was instrumental in ensuring that the Indian Civil Service (ICS) was officially replaced by the Indian Administrative Service (IAS) to serve the needs of a free India.

questions.question: Which of the following Amendments made Education a subject in the Concurrent List?
questions.solution:
Education on Concurrent List

Originally, Education was a subject in the State List. It was moved to the Concurrent List by the 42nd Amendment Act (1976), allowing the Central Government to formulate national policies like the National Education Policy (NEP) while states maintain their own systems.

questions.question: What is the name of the Act that governs the rules and service conditions of All-India Services?
questions.solution:
All-India Services Act, 1951

The All-India Services Act, 1951 is the parent legislation that governs the rules and service conditions for the IAS, IPS, and later the Indian Forest Service (IFS). It empowers the Central Government to frame rules in consultation with State Governments.
